request.question: What island that Zanthoxylum hawaiiense can be found on is nicknamed“The Friendly Isle”?

title: Chelerythrine
Nitidine
Zanthoxylum psammophilum
Molokai
List of islands in Isle Royale National Park
Little Deer Isle, Maine
Zanthoxylum gilletii
Fagaronine
Zanthoxylum hawaiiense
Molokai Island Times
sentences: Chelerythrine is a benzophenanthridine alkaloid present in the plant "Chelidonium majus" (greater celandine).
It is a potent, selective, and cell-permeable protein kinase C inhibitor "in vitro".
And an efficacious antagonist of G-protein-coupled CB1 receptors.
It is also found in the plants "Zanthoxylum clava-herculis" and "Zanthoxylum rhoifolium", exhibiting antibacterial activity against "Staphylococcus aureus" and other human pathogens.
Nitidine is a benzophenanthridine alkaloid found in species of the genus "Zanthoxylum ", notably in "Zanthoxylum nitidum".
This compound has an anti-malarial activity.
Zanthoxylum psammophilum (synonym Fagara psammophila Aké Assi) is a species of shrub or small tree in the Rutaceae family.
It is a large liana endemic to Côte d'Ivoire, although in 2005 it was found in Liberia as well.

"Zanthoxylum psammophilum", a new combination created in 1975 to subsume the genus "Fagara" into the genus "Zanthoxylum" based on morphology and secondary metabolites, is the preferred name according to the Conservatoire et Jardin botaniques de la Ville de Genève which has a section that specializes in the conservation and biodiversity of flowering plants of Côte d'Ivoire.
However, the International Plant Names Index lists "Fagara" as the genus, and "Zanthoxylum" as the synonym.
The type specimen for the plant was collected in the ecotone between the lowland Eastern Guinean forests (tropical rainforest) and the inland Guinean forest-savanna mosaic in the Lagunes District.
Molokai ( ; Hawaiian: ] ), nicknamed “The Friendly Isle” is the fifth largest island of eight major islands that make up the Hawaiian Island Chain in the middle of the Pacific Ocean.

It is 38 by 10 miles (61 by 16 km) in size at its extreme length and width with a usable land area of 260 sqmi , making it the fifth-largest of the main Hawaiian Islands and the 27th largest island in the United States.
It lies east of Oahu across the 25-mile (40 km) wide Kaiwi Channel and north of Lānai, separated from it by the Kalohi Channel.
The following is a list of islands in Isle Royale National Park.
Located in Lake Superior, Isle Royale is the second largest island in the Great Lakes system, after only Manitoulin Island in Lake Huron.
At 206.7 mi² (535.4 km²), Isle Royale is the third largest island in the contiguous United States after Long Island and Padre Island.
The island and the surrounding islands and waters make up Isle Royale National Park for a total area of 209 sq mi² (542 km²) of above-water territory.
Little Deer Isle is an island in Penobscot Bay, lying just north of the town (and island) of Deer Isle, of which it is a part.
The island is served by Maine State Route 15 via the Deer Isle Bridge.
The north end of the island is called Eggemoggin and there is a small island with a lighthouse on it at the tip called Pumpkin Island.
Little Deer is about 4 miles long and connects to Deer Isle by way of a causeway.
There are only about 300 year-round residents of Little Deer, but many more summer residents.
Zanthoxylum gilletii, the East African satin wood, is a tree species in the genus "Zanthoxylum" found in Africa.
The fruits are used to produce the spice uzazi.
Fagaronine is a benzophenanthridine alkaloid found in "Zanthoxylum zanthoxyloides" and other species in the "Zanthoxylum" genus.
Zanthoxylum hawaiiense, commonly known Aʻ e or Hawaiʻ i pricklyash, is a species of flowering plant in the citrus family, Rutaceae, that is endemic to Hawaii.
It can be found at elevations of 550 – in dry forests, where it grows on lava flows, and mixed mesic forests on the Island of Hawaiʻ i, Maui, Molokaʻ i, and Lānaʻ i.
It is threatened by habitat loss.
The Molokai Island Times was one of three newspapers on the Hawaiian island of Moloka'i.

It was founded in December 2004 by Brennan Purtzer and Darrell Williams with a subsidy from computer software guru John McAfee, Williams' English cousin, and served as the newest community paper for "The Friendly Isle" of Molokai from 2005–2006, before becoming known as The Molokai Times in January 2007.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What island that Zanthoxylum hawaiiense can be found on is nicknamed“The Friendly Isle”?
hop1_docs: Zanthoxylum hawaiiense | Zanthoxylum hawaiiense, commonly known Aʻ e or Hawaiʻ i pricklyash, is a species of flowering plant in the citrus family, Rutaceae, that is endemic to Hawaii.  It can be found at elevations of 550 – in dry forests, where it grows on lava flows, and mixed mesic forests on the Island of Hawaiʻ i, Maui, Molokaʻ i, and Lānaʻ i.  It is threatened by habitat loss.

Zanthoxylum thomasianum | Zanthoxylum thomasianum, also known as the St. Thomas prickly-ash, is a species of plant in the Rutaceae family.  It is found in Puerto Rico, the British Virgin Islands, and the United States Virgin Islands.  Its natural habitats are tropical and subtropical dry broadleaf forests and shrublands.  It is threatened by habitat loss, and is the only on St. John listed as "endangered".

Lighthouses on the Isle of Arran | Three lighthouses can be found around the shores of the island of Arran in the Firth of Clyde.  One is located on the Isle of Pladda in the South and the other two can be found on Holy Isle in Lamlash Bay to the East of Arran.  All three are still in service and maintained by the Northern Lighthouse Board.  They are now fully automatic since the electrification of both lighthouses on Holy Isle in 1977.

Zanthoxylum kauaense | Zanthoxylum kauaense, commonly known as Aʻ e or Kauaʻ i pricklyash, is a species of flowering plant in the citrus family, Rutaceae, that is endemic to Hawaii.  It usually inhabits mixed mesic forests at elevations of 300 - , but can also be found in dry and wet forests.  It is threatened by habitat loss.

Zanthoxylum coreanum | The Korean lime tree, Zanthoxylum coreanum Nakai, is a rare shrub that only grows in Korea and China.  In Korea it is called wang-cho-pi tree and is used for many purposes.  There are researches that are done to figure out what compounds are in the "Zanthoxylum coreanum" Nakai and the uses of this plant.

Molokai | Molokai ( ; Hawaiian: ] ), nicknamed “The Friendly Isle” is the fifth largest island of eight major islands that make up the Hawaiian Island Chain in the middle of the Pacific Ocean.  It is 38 by 10 miles (61 by 16 km) in size at its extreme length and width with a usable land area of 260 sqmi , making it the fifth-largest of the main Hawaiian Islands and the 27th largest island in the United States.  It lies east of Oahu across the 25-mile (40 km) wide Kaiwi Channel and north of Lānai, separated from it by the Kalohi Channel.

Shaky Isles | The Shaky Isles or Shaky Islands is a nickname for New Zealand.  At one time this nickname was used in New Zealand itself, though its usage there is now seen as dated; it is still fairly widely used in Australia.

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Zanthoxylum hawaiiense: passage [1] lists it occurring on "Island of Hawaiʻi, Maui, Molokaʻi, and Lānaʻi" (quote: "Island of Hawaiʻi, Maui, Molokaʻi, and Lānaʻi") — [1].
- Molokai island: passage [6] explicitly states the island is "nicknamed “The Friendly Isle”" (quote: "nicknamed “The Friendly Isle”") — [6].

2) Ambiguities:
- Spelling/variant: the plant passage uses "Molokaʻi" (with ʻokina) while the island passage uses "Molokai" (no diacritic). 
- Multiple candidate islands are listed for the plant (Hawaiʻi, Maui, Molokaʻi, Lānaʻi); only Molokai passage in the provided set shows the nickname. No other candidate island pages/variants with that nickname appear in the passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as appears in passages): "Molokaʻi" (from [1]) and "Molokai" (from [6]).
- Precise missing fact needed: explicit confirmation that the "Molokaʻi" listed on the Zanthoxylum hawaiiense page refers to the same island page titled "Molokai" and that that island page's lede/infobox contains the nickname "The Friendly Isle".

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "Zanthoxylum hawaiiense" page (lede/Range/Distribution) and confirm the island list and the exact spelling "Molokaʻi" as used there.
- Open "Molokai" page (lede and infobox; check "Also known as"/"Etymology" or nickname lines) to confirm the nickname "The Friendly Isle" and any alternate spellings (Molokaʻi).
- If a redirect exists, fetch "Molokaʻi" (redirect) or "Moloka'i" page to verify it points to the "Molokai" island article.
- Search keywords if needed: "Molokai nickname Friendly Isle lede infobox", "Molokaʻi island nickname", "Zanthoxylum hawaiiense distribution Molokaʻi".

Final answer withheld until those pages/sections are retrieved.

It can be found at elevations of 550 – in dry forests, where it grows on lava flows, and mixed mesic forests on the Island of Hawaiʻ i, Maui, Molokaʻ i, and Lānaʻ i.  It is threatened by habitat loss.
Kapauanuakea | Kapau-a-Nuʻ akea was a Chiefess of Molokaʻi.  She ruled as a Queen regnant of the island of Molokai.

Molokai Island Times | The Molokai Island Times was one of three newspapers on the Hawaiian island of Moloka'i.  It was founded in December 2004 by Brennan Purtzer and Darrell Williams with a subsidy from computer software guru John McAfee, Williams' English cousin, and served as the newest community paper for "The Friendly Isle" of Molokai from 2005–2006, before becoming known as The Molokai Times in January 2007.

Molokai | Molokai ( ; Hawaiian: ] ), nicknamed “The Friendly Isle” is the fifth largest island of eight major islands that make up the Hawaiian Island Chain in the middle of the Pacific Ocean.  It is 38 by 10 miles (61 by 16 km) in size at its extreme length and width with a usable land area of 260 sqmi , making it the fifth-largest of the main Hawaiian Islands and the 27th largest island in the United States.  It lies east of Oahu across the 25-mile (40 km) wide Kaiwi Channel and north of Lānai, separated from it by the Kalohi Channel.

Aspidodiadema hawaiiense | Aspidodiadema hawaiiense is a species of sea urchin of the family Aspidodiadematidae.  Their armor is covered with spines.  It is placed in the genus Aspidodiadema and lives in the sea.  Aspidodiadema hawaiiense was first scientifically described in 1939 by Ole Theodor Jensen Mortensen, a Danish scientist.

Xylosma hawaiiense | Xylosma hawaiiense is a species of flowering plant in the willow family, Salicaceae, that is endemic to Hawaii.  Common names include Hawai'i brushholly, maua, and aʻ e (Maui only).

Bidens wiebkei | Wiebke's beggarticks or Molokaʻi Koʻokoʻolau (Bidens wiebkei) is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae.  It belongs to the genus "Bidens", collectively called "kokoʻolau" or "koʻokoʻolau" in the Hawaiian language.  It is found only on Molokaʻi in the Hawaiian Islands.  There are three occurrences of the plant remaining, for a total population of fewer than 1000.
